fig_p24_003_vec.png.title: The Origins and Core Principles of Technical Analysis
fig_p24_003_vec.png.description: An informational text box detailing the historical origins of technical analysis, focusing on Charles Dow's foundational observations (Dow theory) and the subsequent expansion by Robert D. Edwards and John Magee in their 1948 book 'Technical Analysis of Stock Trends'.
knowledge_claims: Charles Dow observed that security prices move in trends, which can be identified using patterns and support/resistance trendlines.
Primary trends (lasting months or years) are punctuated by secondary movements (retracements) in the opposite direction.
Trends remain in place until a major event stops them.

Robert D. Edwards and John Magee's 1948 book 'Technical Analysis of Stock Trends' was the first major book to use the term 'technical analysis' in its title and formalized many core concepts still used today.

fig_p32_004.png.title: Downtrend, Key Reversal, and Uptrend Chart
fig_p32_004.png.description: A technical analysis price chart illustrating the transition of a security's price from a downward trend to an upward trend. The left portion shows a declining price pattern labeled 'DOWNTREND' with a downward-sloping grey trendline. At the lowest point of the chart, a circle highlights a sharp price dip labeled 'KEY REVERSAL'. To the right of this reversal point, the price rises consistently, labeled 'UPTREND' with an upward-sloping grey trendline.
knowledge_claims: A downtrend is characterized by a sustained downward movement in price over time.
A key reversal represents a pivotal turning point where a downward price trend ends and an upward trend begins.
An uptrend is characterized by a sustained upward movement in price following a reversal event.
Trendlines can be used to visually simplify and define the direction of market trends.

fig_p39_005_vec.png.title: Artificial Intelligence in Algorithmic Trading
fig_p39_005_vec.png.description: A text excerpt explaining how algorithmic trading systems transition into artificial intelligence (AI) through machine learning and feedback loops. It discusses the adaptability of AI to market volatility, the simulation of non-price factors, the scale of quantitative trading in the U.S. equity market, and the critical limitation of AI regarding spurious correlations and the necessity of human-designed feedback protocols.
knowledge_claims: AI makes technical trading systems more adaptive by modifying performance rules using feedback without human intervention.
Advanced AI systems can simulate price changes in nanoseconds by calculating the probabilities of dozens of non-price factors like economic data and central bank policy changes.
In early 2019, JP Morgan estimated that quant strategies managed at least $1.5 trillion in the U.S. equity market, and traditional non-quant investors accounted for only about 10 percent of trading.
AI systems cannot determine whether a correlation makes logical sense, meaning they require human-designed feedback protocols to avoid acting on spurious correlations.

fig_p53_006.png.title: Resistance Line on an Upward Trend Price Chart
fig_p53_006.png.description: An OHLC (Open-High-Low-Close) bar chart showing a stock's price movement over time with vertical grid lines. A solid blue diagonal line is drawn across the top peaks of the price action, representing an ascending resistance line where the price repeatedly struggles to break through and subsequently pulls back.
knowledge_claims: A resistance line can be drawn by connecting the consecutive peaks of a security's price action.
The resistance line represents a price level where selling pressure or lack of demand typically halts upward movement.
In an uptrend, the resistance line itself can be diagonal and ascending, showing that the resistance level increases over time.

fig_p53_007.png.title: Downward Trending Channel with Support and Resistance Lines
fig_p53_007.png.description: A financial bar chart illustrating a downward trending price range, also known as a descending channel. Two parallel blue lines represent the upper resistance level and the lower support level, containing the fluctuating price bars. The overall trajectory of the price action is sloping downwards, indicating a bearish trend where highs and lows are progressively lower over time.
knowledge_claims: A descending channel is defined by parallel downward-sloping support and resistance lines.
The upper line acts as resistance where selling pressure increases, while the lower line acts as support where buying pressure increases.
A downward-sloping channel indicates a market environment where traders are willing to sell at progressively lower highs, signaling a bearish trend.

fig_p65_008.png.title: Uptrend with Retracements and Support Levels
fig_p65_008.png.description: An OHLC bar chart illustrating a security in a steady uptrend. A thick grey diagonal trendline is drawn along the bottom of the price action. Four temporary price pullbacks (retracements) are highlighted with black ovals, showing how the price periodically dips before resuming its upward trajectory. Horizontal lines extend from the lows of these retracements to demonstrate how previous price levels act as support.
knowledge_claims: An uptrend consists of higher highs and higher lows, punctuated by temporary downward price retracements.
Retracements represent temporary profit-taking or liquidity exhaustion before the primary trend resumes.
Prior retracement lows can establish horizontal support levels that help traders identify potential entry points or stop-loss levels.

fig_p81_009.png.title: Stock Price Chart with Volume and On-Balance Volume (OBV) Analysis
fig_p81_009.png.description: A multi-panel financial chart spanning 2012 to 2013. The top panel displays stock price action with a red uptrend line that gets broken, followed by a double bottom pattern highlighted by two circles and a horizontal resistance line. The middle panel shows daily trading volume with arrows indicating volume trends and a circle highlighting a volume spike on the first bottom. The bottom panel displays the On-Balance Volume (OBV) indicator as a continuous red line reflecting cumulative volume flow.
knowledge_claims: A break below the upward trendline (red line) signals a reversal of the primary bullish trend.
The double bottom pattern (highlighted by circles on the price chart) is confirmed by a breakout above the horizontal resistance line.
Volume analysis (middle panel) shows a significant volume spike during the formation of the first bottom, indicating strong buying interest or capitulation at that support level.
The On-Balance Volume (OBV) indicator in the bottom panel helps confirm trend strength and potential reversals by tracking cumulative volume flow.

fig_p100_010.png.title: Downtrend Channel with Multiple Internal Swings
fig_p100_010.png.description: A candlestick chart showing a clear downward-sloping price channel defined by three parallel blue lines representing the upper, middle, and lower boundaries. The price action oscillates between these boundaries, creating four distinct downward legs and four upward corrective legs within the overall primary downtrend.
knowledge_claims: A primary downtrend is often composed of multiple smaller-scale upward and downward price swings.
Parallel trendlines can define a channel that acts as dynamic support and resistance for price action.
Traders can approach a channeled trend either by holding a single long-term position or by trading the individual swings within the channel boundaries.

fig_p101_011_vec.png.title: Fading the Trend Trading Strategy
fig_p101_011_vec.png.description: An informational text box explaining the concept of 'fading the trend' in financial trading. It describes how traders can exploit short-term retracements within an established uptrend by taking short positions, noting that this countertrend strategy is highly reliable but works best over very short timeframes like hours or days.
knowledge_claims: Prices often move in a 'two steps forward, one step back' pattern, creating predictable pauses or retracements in an uptrend.
Fading the trend involves selling a long position and going short to exploit a temporary downward retracement within an established uptrend.
Trading retracements (fading the trend) is a standard operating procedure, particularly in futures markets.
Fading the trend is most effective when executed as a very short-term trade lasting only hours or days.

fig_p134_012.png.title: Parabolic Stop-and-Reverse (SAR) Model
fig_p134_012.png.description: A financial price chart illustrating the parabolic stop-and-reverse (SAR) indicator. It shows a series of vertical price bars in an uptrend with red dots plotted underneath them, representing the trailing stop-loss level. The chart highlights a 'BUY HERE' entry point at the start of the trend and a 'SELL HERE' exit point where the price action falters and converges with the stop-loss dot.
knowledge_claims: The red dots represent a dynamic, self-adjusting stop-loss level that rises as long as the price continues to increase.
A buy signal is initiated at the start of the uptrend when the price moves above the initial stop-loss level.
When the upward momentum slows down, the price converges with the stop-loss dot, triggering a sell signal to exit the trade.

fig_p148_013.png.title: Anatomy of a Standard Price Bar (OHLC)
fig_p148_013.png.description: This diagram illustrates the four components of a standard OHLC (Open-High-Low-Close) price bar. A central vertical line represents the price range, with the top labeled 'HIGH' and the bottom labeled 'LOW'. A horizontal tick mark on the left side of the vertical line is labeled 'OPEN' with an arrow pointing to it, and a horizontal tick mark on the right side is labeled 'CLOSE' with an arrow pointing to it.
knowledge_claims: The vertical line of a price bar represents the total price range between the high and low prices of the trading period.
The horizontal tick mark on the left side of the vertical line indicates the opening price.
The horizontal tick mark on the right side of the vertical line indicates the closing price.

fig_p154_014.png.title: OHLC Bar Chart Sequence with Highlighted Day 4
fig_p154_014.png.description: A sequence of five OHLC (Open-High-Low-Close) bar charts representing consecutive trading days. Each bar consists of a vertical line showing the high and low price range, a left-facing horizontal tick representing the opening price, and a right-facing horizontal tick representing the closing price. An arrow points to 'DAY 4', which shows a significantly smaller trading range and lower volatility compared to the other days in the sequence.
knowledge_claims: An OHLC bar uses a vertical line to represent the high-to-low price range of a trading period, with left and right horizontal ticks representing the open and close prices respectively.
Day 4 represents a contraction in trading range (low volatility) relative to the surrounding days.
The overall five-day sequence demonstrates an upward price trend, with the closing price of Day 5 being substantially higher than the opening price of Day 1.

fig_p155_015.png.title: OHLC Bar Chart Illustrating a Series of Down-Days
fig_p155_015.png.description: This diagram shows five sequential OHLC (Open-High-Low-Close) price bars depicting a downward trend. Each vertical line represents the daily price range, with a left horizontal tick indicating the opening price and a right horizontal tick indicating the closing price. An arrow labeled 'DAY 3' points to the third bar, marking the start of a consecutive series of days where each day's closing price is lower than the previous day's close.
knowledge_claims: An OHLC bar represents the daily price range with a vertical line, the opening price with a left-facing tick, and the closing price with a right-facing tick.
A series of down-days is defined by consecutive days where each closing price is lower than the previous day's closing price.
Day 3 in the diagram initiates a downward trend where sellers are willing to accept lower prices to exit their positions.

fig_p157_016_vec.png.title: Current Events: Buy on the Rumor, Sell on the News
fig_p157_016_vec.png.description: An educational text sidebar explaining the concept of event risk and the market phenomenon of 'buy on the rumor, sell on the news'. It categorizes market events into scheduled and unexpected developments, and details how stock prices react before and after news releases based on market expectations.
key_concepts: Buy on the Rumor, Sell on the News
knowledge_claims: Event risk is the risk that a new high or low price will ensue from a specific piece of news, which can be either scheduled or unexpected.
Traders 'build in' forecasts to a stock's price before an event occurs, which is known as 'buying on the rumor'.
The 'sell on the news' phenomenon occurs when a stock price falls immediately after an event because early buyers take profits, even if the news matches the forecast.
If news is significantly better than the forecast, prices may continue to rise; if it fails to meet expectations, a longer-lasting price drop typically ensues.

fig_p163_017.png.title: OHLC Bar Chart Showing Price Action and Trend Reversals
fig_p163_017.png.description: An OHLC (Open-High-Low-Close) bar chart tracking price movements from late March through mid-April. The vertical axis on the right displays price levels from 0.710 to 0.745, while the horizontal axis marks dates including March 24, March 31, and April 7, 14, and 21. The chart features alternating light gray and black price bars, highlighting a sharp downward trend in early April that bottoms out with a long lower shadow on April 7 before recovering.
knowledge_claims: The chart illustrates an OHLC bar format where left ticks represent opening prices and right ticks represent closing prices.
A significant downward price spike on April 7th represents a sharp intraday low near 0.712 followed by a close significantly higher, indicating strong buying pressure or support at that level.
The color coding of the bars (light gray vs. black) helps distinguish between upward and downward price movements or different trading sessions.

fig_p169_018.png.title: Fitschen's Simple Bar-Scoring Types
fig_p169_018.png.description: An illustration of eight different bar-type configurations used in Fitschen's simple bar-scoring system. Each of the eight panels (Bar-Type 1 to Bar-Type 8) depicts two vertical lines representing consecutive trading days, with horizontal ticks indicating open and close levels. The diagrams visually represent combinations of whether the close is above or below the open, whether the close is in the upper or lower half of the daily range, and how the current close compares to the preceding day's close.
knowledge_claims: Fitschen's bar-scoring system classifies daily price bars into eight distinct types based on the relationship between open, close, range, and the previous day's close.
Each bar-type diagram displays a two-day sequence to compare the current day's price action against the preceding day's close.
These eight bar-types are used to analyze and predict subsequent market performance in equities and futures.

fig_p174_019.png.title: Common Special Price Bar Patterns
fig_p174_019.png.description: An illustration of four distinct price bar configurations labeled A, B, C, and D, representing common special bar patterns in technical analysis. Pattern A shows a series of upward-trending bars ending in a very tall bar. Pattern B shows a large bar followed by smaller bars nested within its vertical range (inside bars). Pattern C shows a large bar engulfing the range of the previous smaller bars (outside bar). Pattern D shows a sequence of narrow-range bars with horizontal ticks near the center, indicating indecision.
knowledge_claims: Pattern B represents an inside bar pattern where subsequent price ranges are entirely contained within the range of a preceding large bar, indicating market indecision.
Pattern C represents an outside bar pattern where a single bar's high-low range completely engulfs the range of the preceding bar, indicating a strong expansion in volatility.
Pattern D illustrates narrow-range bars with opens and closes near the middle of the range, signifying low volatility and market equilibrium or indecision.
Pattern A shows a strong directional trend culminating in an abnormally large trading range bar, indicating high momentum or a sudden shift in trader sentiment.

fig_p177_020.png.title: Spike Low and Key Reversal Price Bar Patterns
fig_p177_020.png.description: The image consists of two panels illustrating uncommon price bar patterns known as spikes. The top panel shows a series of rising price bars followed by an exceptionally long bar extending downwards, labeled as a 'Spike Low'. The bottom panel shows a 'Key Reversal' pattern where a very tall price bar extends both above and below the surrounding bars, with a label pointing to the bottom tick indicating 'Exit at Spike Low'.
knowledge_claims: A spike low is characterized by an exceptionally long price bar that extends significantly below the preceding price action.
A key reversal bar features an extremely wide high-low range that stands out dramatically from surrounding bars, signaling a potential shift in market direction.
The low point of a key reversal spike can serve as a critical exit signal ('Exit at Spike Low') for traders.

fig_p180_021.png.title: Illustration of a Price Gap on a Bar Chart
fig_p180_021.png.description: A schematic diagram illustrating a price gap in technical analysis. It shows two sets of vertical price bars: a lower set on the left and a higher set on the right. A clear horizontal void labeled 'GAP' is highlighted between two grey lines, showing that the lowest price of the second group does not overlap with the highest price of the first group.
knowledge_claims: A gap is a visible discontinuity between two consecutive price bars on a chart.
A gap represents a price range where no transactions took place due to a sudden shift in supply and demand.
An upward gap occurs when the low of the current bar is higher than the high of the previous bar.

fig_p184_022.png.title: Breakaway Gap, Pullback, and Runaway Gap Chart Pattern
fig_p184_022.png.description: A conceptual bar chart diagram illustrating the sequence of a breakaway gap, a subsequent pullback, and a runaway gap. The breakaway gap marks the initial jump in price out of a consolidation range. Following a brief upward move, a pullback occurs (highlighted by an oval) where prices temporarily dip. The upward trend then resumes, leading to a runaway gap that signals strong continuation of the trend.
knowledge_claims: A breakaway gap occurs when the price breaks out of a trading range or consolidation area, signaling the start of a new trend.
A pullback is a temporary price retracement that occurs after the initial breakout before the primary trend resumes.
A runaway gap occurs in the middle of a strong trend, indicating intense interest and continuation of the prevailing direction.

fig_p187_023.png.title: Island Reversal Pattern
fig_p187_023.png.description: A schematic diagram illustrating an island reversal pattern in technical analysis. It shows an upward trend of price bars leading to a 'GAP UP', which isolates a single high price bar labeled 'ISLAND' at the top. This is immediately followed by a 'GAP DOWN' leading to a downward trend of price bars, leaving the top bar completely separated from the rest of the price action.
knowledge_claims: An island reversal is characterized by an isolated price bar or group of bars separated from the main body of price action by gaps on both sides.
The pattern begins with a gap up (exhaustion gap) and is completed by a subsequent gap down (breakaway gap) in the opposite direction.
This pattern represents a strong signal of a trend reversal from bullish to bearish.

fig_p189_024.png.title: Filling a Gap in Stock Trading
fig_p189_024.png.description: A schematic bar chart illustrating the concept of 'filling a gap' in technical analysis. The chart shows an initial upward price trend that gaps upward, leaving an empty price range bounded by two horizontal black lines. Subsequent price bars decline back into this empty range, highlighted by a circle labeled 'FILLING A GAP', before the price resumes its upward trajectory.
knowledge_claims: Filling a gap occurs when subsequent price action retraces back into the empty price range created by a previous gap.
The upper and lower boundaries of a gap represent key price levels that the market may retest.
After a gap is filled, the price often resumes its original trend direction.

fig_p191_025.png.title: Range Expansion and Contraction in Price Bars
fig_p191_025.png.description: A conceptual diagram illustrating two types of trading range behaviors over time. Chart A shows a sequence of vertical price bars that increase in length from left to right, representing range expansion. Chart B shows a sequence of vertical price bars that decrease in length from left to right, representing range contraction.
knowledge_claims: Range expansion is represented by a lengthening of high-low price bars over time and typically suggests a continuation pattern.
Range contraction is represented by a shortening of high-low price bars over time and suggests that a trend reversal may be approaching.
A change in the size of the high-low range often precedes or accompanies a change in the direction or slope of a trend.

fig_p194_026.png.title: The Problem of Gaps in Average Range Calculation
fig_p194_026.png.description: A simplified vertical bar chart illustrating two consecutive trading days with a price gap. Day 1 has a price range from 1 to 3 (a range of $2.00), and Day 2 has a price range from 5 to 7 (a range of $2.00). Although there is a gap of $2.00 between the close of Day 1 and the open of Day 2, a standard average range calculation of these two days results in an average range of $2.00, demonstrating how standard calculations fail to account for price gaps.
knowledge_claims: Day 1 has a trading range of $2.00 (from 1 to 3) and Day 2 has a trading range of $2.00 (from 5 to 7).
A gap exists between the high of Day 1 ($3.00) and the low of Day 2 ($5.00).
A simple average of the daily ranges yields $2.00, which fails to capture the true price movement because it ignores the gap between the two days.

fig_p196_027.png.title: Calculation of Average True Range with a Price Gap
fig_p196_027.png.description: This diagram illustrates how to calculate the True Range and Average True Range (ATR) when a price gap occurs between two trading days. Day 1 has a price range of $1.00 to $3.00 (Range = $2.00). Day 2 has a price range of $5.00 to $7.00 (Range = $2.00). To account for the gap, the Day 2 True Range is calculated from Day 1's close ($3.00) to Day 2's high ($7.00), resulting in a True Range of $4.00. The Average True Range is the average of these two values: ($2.00 + $4.00) / 2 = $3.00.
knowledge_claims: A standard daily range calculation ($5.00 to $7.00 = $2.00) fails to capture the volatility introduced by an overnight price gap.
The True Range for a day following a gap is calculated from the previous day's close to the current day's high (or low, depending on direction).
In this example, incorporating the gap increases the Day 2 True Range from $2.00 to $4.00.
The Average True Range over the two-day period is calculated as $3.00 by averaging the Day 1 range ($2.00) and the Day 2 True Range ($4.00).

fig_p197_028.png.title: Change in ATR as a Trend Reversal Warning Indicator
fig_p197_028.png.description: A dual-panel financial chart showing price bars on the bottom and the Average True Range (ATR) indicator on the top. The price is in an uptrend (indicated by a blue support line) while the ATR is in a downtrend (indicated by a blue resistance line). A black circle highlights the point where the price trend ends and ATR reaches a low. Immediately after, a 'BIG-BAR DOWN DAY' in price corresponds to a sharp 'JUMP IN ATR ON BIG-BAR DOWN DAY' in the top panel.
knowledge_claims: A falling Average True Range (ATR) during a price uptrend can signal a weakening trend and potential reversal.
A sharp increase in ATR confirms a strong new directional move, such as a high-volatility downward breakout.
ATR is a non-directional measure of volatility, meaning it rises during sharp downward price moves just as it would during sharp upward moves.

fig_p201_029.png.title: Anatomy of Candlestick Bars
fig_p201_029.png.description: This diagram illustrates the components of both a bullish (white) and a bearish (black) candlestick bar. It labels the 'High' and 'Low' price points at the tips of the upper and lower shadows, respectively. The 'Real Body' represents the range between the 'Open' and 'Close' prices, where a white body indicates the close is higher than the open, and a black body indicates the open is higher than the close.
knowledge_claims: The real body of a candlestick represents the price range between the open and close of a trading period.
The thin lines extending above and below the real body are called shadows (or wicks) and represent the high and low prices of the period.
A white (or hollow) candlestick indicates a bullish period where the close price is higher than the open price.
A black (or filled) candlestick indicates a bearish period where the close price is lower than the open price.

fig_p203_030.png.title: Types of Doji Candlesticks
fig_p203_030.png.description: An illustration of three distinct variations of the Doji candlestick pattern used in technical analysis: Plain Doji, Dragonfly Doji, and Gravestone Doji. The Plain Doji is shaped like a cross, indicating that the open and close prices are equal and located near the middle of the high-low range. The Dragonfly Doji is shaped like a 'T', showing that the open, high, and close prices are all at the top of the range, with a long lower shadow. The Gravestone Doji is shaped like an inverted 'T', showing that the open, low, and close prices are at the bottom of the range, with a long upper shadow.
knowledge_claims: A Doji candlestick represents a session where the opening and closing prices are virtually identical, resulting in little to no real body.
A Plain Doji has upper and lower shadows of roughly equal length, indicating market indecision.
A Dragonfly Doji has a long lower shadow and no upper shadow, indicating that sellers pushed prices down but buyers drove them back up to the open/high level by the close.
A Gravestone Doji has a long upper shadow and no lower shadow, indicating that buyers pushed prices up but sellers drove them back down to the open/low level by the close.

fig_p205_031.png.title: Shaven Top and Shaven Bottom Candlestick Patterns
fig_p205_031.png.description: An illustration of two candlestick patterns with missing shadows. The left diagram shows a 'SHAVEN TOP' candlestick, which features a rectangular real body with a lower shadow (wick) but no upper shadow. The right diagram shows a 'SHAVEN BOTTOM' candlestick, which features a rectangular real body with an upper shadow (wick) but no lower shadow.
knowledge_claims: A shaven top candlestick pattern is characterized by the complete absence of an upper shadow, meaning the high of the session is equal to either the open or the close.
A shaven bottom candlestick pattern is characterized by the complete absence of a lower shadow, meaning the low of the session is equal to either the open or the close.

fig_p206_032.png.title: Candlestick Patterns with Long Upper and Lower Shadows
fig_p206_032.png.description: An illustration of two candlestick patterns demonstrating extreme price shadows. The left candlestick shows a rectangular real body with a long vertical line extending upwards, labeled 'LONG UPPER SHADOW'. The right candlestick shows a rectangular real body with a long vertical line extending downwards, labeled 'LONG LOWER SHADOW'.
knowledge_claims: A long upper shadow indicates that the high of the trading period was significantly higher than both the open and close prices.
A long lower shadow indicates that the low of the trading period was significantly lower than both the open and close prices.
Long shadows represent sentiment extremes where traders pushed prices far from the opening/closing range but failed to sustain those levels.

fig_p208_033.png.title: Sudden Range Expansion Indicating Potential Resistance
fig_p208_033.png.description: The image displays two parallel representations of an upward price trend: a candlestick chart on top and a bar chart on the bottom. Both show a series of small, steady upward movements followed by a sudden, exceptionally large bullish bar (range expansion). An arrow points to the top of this large bar with the label 'RESISTANCE?', illustrating how a sudden surge in price range can signal that a resistance level has been reached.
knowledge_claims: A sudden, exceptionally long bar or candlestick following a series of smaller, steady bars can indicate that the price has reached a significant resistance level.
Range expansion at the end of a trend often represents an emotional climax or extreme buying exhaustion, marking a potential turning point.
The peak of a sudden large upward price bar serves as a key level to watch for potential resistance and trend deceleration.

fig_p210_034.png.title: Hammer and Hanging Man Candlestick Patterns
fig_p210_034.png.description: This diagram illustrates two classic candlestick patterns: the Hammer and the Hanging Man. The top panel shows a downtrend of black candlesticks ending with a Hammer candlestick (characterized by a small white body and a long lower shadow), which precedes a bullish reversal. The bottom panel shows an uptrend of white candlesticks ending with a Hanging Man candlestick (characterized by a small black body and a long lower shadow), which precedes a bearish reversal.
knowledge_claims: A Hammer candlestick pattern appears at the bottom of a downtrend and signals a potential bullish trend reversal.
A Hanging Man candlestick pattern appears at the top of an uptrend and signals a potential bearish trend reversal.
Both Hammer and Hanging Man patterns feature a small real body near the top of the price range and a long lower shadow.
The context of the preceding trend (downtrend for Hammer, uptrend for Hanging Man) is critical to interpreting these visually similar candlesticks.

fig_p212_035.png.title: Harami Candlestick Pattern
fig_p212_035.png.description: An illustration of a Harami candlestick pattern, which consists of two bars. The first bar is a long white (hollow) candlestick, and the second bar is a smaller black (filled) candlestick whose real body is completely contained within the vertical range of the first bar's body, as indicated by the bracket above them.
knowledge_claims: A Harami pattern is a two-bar candlestick pattern where a small real-body candlestick follows a much larger real-body candlestick.
The second candlestick's real body must be completely contained within the range of the first candlestick's real body.
The Harami pattern indicates an impending change or pause in market sentiment.

fig_p214_036.png.title: Bearish Engulfing Candlestick and Shooting Star Patterns
fig_p214_036.png.description: This diagram illustrates two common bearish reversal candlestick patterns used in technical analysis. The top section shows a 'Bearish Engulfing Candlestick (Two Bars)' pattern, where a large black (bearish) candle body completely covers the preceding smaller white (bullish) candle body. The bottom section shows a 'Shooting Star' pattern, which features a small real body at the lower end of the price range with a very long upper shadow (wick) following an upward trend.
knowledge_claims: A bearish engulfing pattern is a two-bar reversal signal where a large black candle's body completely engulfs the body of the preceding white candle.
A shooting star is a single-bar bearish reversal pattern characterized by a small real body near the bottom of the session's range and a long upper shadow.
Both patterns indicate a potential shift in momentum from buyers (bulls) to sellers (bears) at the peak of an uptrend.

fig_p216_037.png.title: Continuation and Trend Candlestick Patterns
fig_p216_037.png.description: This diagram illustrates three common candlestick patterns used in technical analysis: Rising Window, Three White Soldiers, and Three Black Crows. The Rising Window shows an upward gap between two white candlesticks that remains unfilled. Three White Soldiers consists of three consecutive ascending white candlesticks, and Three Black Crows consists of three consecutive descending black candlesticks.
knowledge_claims: A Rising Window pattern features an upward gap between candlesticks that acts as a bullish continuation signal when the gap remains unfilled.
The Three White Soldiers pattern consists of three consecutive, strong bullish (white) candlesticks that confirm a strong upward trend.
The Three Black Crows pattern consists of three consecutive, strong bearish (black) candlesticks indicating a strong downward trend or reversal.

fig_p218_038.png.title: Candlestick Patterns in an Ascending Channel
fig_p218_038.png.description: This candlestick chart demonstrates how various candlestick patterns appear within an ascending price channel bounded by parallel support and resistance lines. It highlights a Harami pattern at the bottom-left support line, followed by a Rising Window (gap up), a Doji representing indecision in the middle of the trend, and an Engulfing Bearish Candle near the upper resistance line signaling a potential reversal.
knowledge_claims: A Harami pattern followed by a rising window near the lower channel line indicates a strong bullish reversal and continuation.
A Doji candle within the channel represents a temporary pause or indecision in the prevailing uptrend.
An engulfing bearish candle near the upper channel line signals strong resistance and a potential downward reversal.

fig_p226_039.png.title: Simplified Price Bar Chart Pattern
fig_p226_039.png.description: A simplified technical analysis chart showing thirteen vertical lines representing price bars (high-low ranges) over time. The bars trace a downward trend to a low point, a brief upward retracement, a second decline to a slightly higher low, and a subsequent upward movement, illustrating a classic double bottom or trend reversal pattern.
knowledge_claims: Vertical lines represent the price range (high to low) for a given trading period without open/close ticks.
The sequence of price bars illustrates a transition from a downtrend to a potential uptrend through a double-bottom-like structure.
Simplified bar charts are used in technical analysis to focus purely on high-low price action and pattern recognition.

fig_p227_040.png.title: Symmetrical Triangle Chart Pattern with Downside Breakout
fig_p227_040.png.description: A schematic diagram illustrating a symmetrical triangle chart pattern, also known as a coil. It features two converging trendlines: a downward-sloping upper line connecting lower highs and an upward-sloping lower line connecting higher lows. Inside, vertical price bars show decreasing volatility. An arrow points to a price bar that has broken below the lower trendline, labeled 'BREAKOUT'.
knowledge_claims: A symmetrical triangle pattern is characterized by converging upper and lower trendlines that represent lower highs and higher lows.
A breakout occurs when the price action pierces through one of the established trendlines, signaling a potential new trend direction.
The narrowing price range within the triangle indicates a period of market consolidation before a decisive breakout.

fig_p230_041.png.title: Ascending and Descending Triangle Chart Patterns
fig_p230_041.png.description: This diagram illustrates two common technical analysis chart patterns: the Ascending Triangle and the Descending Triangle. The Ascending Triangle features a flat horizontal resistance line at the top and an upward-sloping support line at the bottom, showing price bars consolidating before breaking out upward. The Descending Triangle features a flat horizontal support line at the bottom and a downward-sloping resistance line at the top, showing price bars consolidating before breaking out downward.
knowledge_claims: An ascending triangle pattern is formed by a flat upper resistance level and rising lower lows, typically resolving in an upward bullish breakout.
A descending triangle pattern is formed by a flat lower support level and falling upper highs, typically resolving in a downward bearish breakout.
Both triangle patterns represent a period of price consolidation where the range narrows before a decisive breakout occurs.

fig_p232_042.png.title: The Dead-Cat Bounce Pattern
fig_p232_042.png.description: A simplified price bar chart illustrating the dead-cat bounce continuation pattern. It shows an initial downward trend followed by a sharp downward gap labeled 'BREAKAWAY GAP'. After further decline, the price experiences a temporary upward retracement labeled 'BOUNCE' before resuming its downward trajectory to new lows.
knowledge_claims: A dead-cat bounce is a continuation pattern where a temporary price recovery occurs during a major decline.
The pattern is often preceded by a sharp downward gap known as a breakaway gap.
The temporary upward bounce eventually fails, and the prevailing downward trend resumes.

fig_p233_043.png.title: Double Bottom Chart Pattern
fig_p233_043.png.description: A schematic diagram illustrating the classic 'Double Bottom' bullish reversal pattern, which resembles the letter 'W'. The diagram highlights two price lows at a similar level, a horizontal resistance line drawn across the intervening peak, a 'Confirmation Point' where the price breaks above this resistance line, and a subsequent 'Pullback' where the price temporarily retraces to the breakout level before continuing its upward trend.
knowledge_claims: A double bottom pattern is a bullish reversal pattern characterized by two distinct price lows at approximately the same level, forming a 'W' shape.
The confirmation point of a double bottom pattern occurs when the price breaks above the peak level between the two bottoms.
Following the breakout at the confirmation point, a pullback often occurs where the price temporarily dips back to the breakout line before resuming its upward trajectory.

fig_p237_044.png.title: Head-and-Shoulders Chart Pattern and Neckline Confirmation
fig_p237_044.png.description: This diagram illustrates two variations of the classic head-and-shoulders topping pattern used in technical analysis. The top illustration shows a clean, simplified version with an upward-sloping neckline connecting the two intermediate troughs. The bottom illustration shows a more realistic, jagged price-action version with a downward-sloping neckline. In both cases, the neckline acts as a support level, and its breach (labeled as confirmation) signals a trend reversal.
knowledge_claims: The head-and-shoulders pattern consists of three peaks, where the middle peak (the head) is higher than the two surrounding peaks (the shoulders).
The neckline is a support line drawn by connecting the low points (troughs) between the head and the shoulders.
A breakout below the neckline serves as the confirmation signal that the upward trend has reversed.
The neckline of a head-and-shoulders pattern can be either upward-sloping or downward-sloping.

fig_p240_045.png.title: Measured Move Price Targets
fig_p240_045.png.description: This diagram illustrates three different methods for calculating a measured move price target in technical analysis. The top diagram shows a price target projected upward from a consolidation pattern breakout. The middle diagram shows an ABCD pattern within parallel trendlines, where the distance from A to B is projected from C to find target D. The bottom diagram shows a gap-based measured move, where the gap represents the midpoint of the entire price move, projecting an equal distance above the gap to find the target.
knowledge_claims: A measured move price target can be calculated by projecting the height of a consolidation pattern upward from the breakout point.
In an ABCD channel pattern, the price target D is estimated by projecting the length of the initial move (A to B) from the retracement low (C).
When a gap occurs in a strong trend, the gap can act as the midpoint of the total move, allowing traders to project an equal distance upward to find the price target.

fig_p246_046.png.title: Candlestick Chart with Zigzag Indicator Overlay
fig_p246_046.png.description: A financial candlestick chart showing price fluctuations over time, overlaid with a red zigzag line. The red line connects significant peaks and troughs, filtering out minor price movements (noise) based on a specific percentage reversal threshold. The chart illustrates how the zigzag indicator simplifies trend visualization by highlighting major trend reversals.
knowledge_claims: The zigzag indicator filters out minor price fluctuations to highlight major trend changes and structural peaks/troughs.
The indicator connects local extrema (highs and lows) with straight lines based on a predefined percentage reversal threshold.
While visually clean in hindsight, the zigzag indicator's lag and sensitivity to volatility changes make real-time application challenging.

fig_p248_047.png.title: Drawing and Trading with a Support Line
fig_p248_047.png.description: A daily stock price chart from September to December showing an uptrend with a blue support line drawn along the price lows. The chart features two key annotations: 'BUY ON THIRD TOUCH OF SUPPORT' pointing to the third contact point on the trendline in late October, and 'SELL ON BREAK OF SUPPORT LINE' pointing to the point where the price breaks below the trendline in mid-November. Volume bars are displayed at the bottom of the chart.
knowledge_claims: A support line is established by connecting consecutive higher lows during an upward price trend.
The third touch of a support line serves as a confirmation of the trend and acts as a buy signal.
A break below the support line indicates a trend reversal or weakness, serving as a sell signal.

fig_p252_048.png.title: Trading a Downtrend Using Resistance Lines
fig_p252_048.png.description: A daily stock price chart spanning from April to October showing a clear downtrend with descending blue resistance lines. The chart illustrates a trading strategy with annotations pointing to specific price action events: 'SELL ON THIRD TOUCH OF RESISTANCE' at the third peak touching the upper resistance line in April and the lower resistance line in August, and 'CLOSE SHORT POSITION WHEN RESISTANCE IS BROKEN TO THE UPSIDE' in mid-September when the price breaks above the lower resistance line.
knowledge_claims: A descending resistance line is drawn by connecting consecutive lower highs in a downtrend.
The third touch of a descending resistance line serves as a technical entry signal to initiate a short position.
A short position should be closed immediately when the price breaks above the descending resistance line to the upside, indicating a potential trend reversal.

fig_p254_049.png.title: The 1-2-3 Trend Reversal Rule
fig_p254_049.png.description: A stylized stock chart illustrating Victor Sperandeo's 1-2-3 Rule for identifying a trend reversal. The chart shows an uptrending price series with a diagonal support line. The reversal is marked by three sequential events: first, the support line is broken; second, the price rallies to test the highest high but fails to exceed it; third, the price falls below the previous significant low, confirming the downtrend.
knowledge_claims: A trend reversal begins with the breaking of an established upward support line.
Following the support line break, a test of the highest high occurs where the price fails to make a new high.
The trend reversal is officially confirmed when the price falls below the previous significant low.

fig_p259_050.png.title: Linear Regression Line on a Price Chart with Deviations
fig_p259_050.png.description: An OHLC bar chart showing price fluctuations over time with a blue linear regression line drawn through the middle section. Label A points to a peak where the price is significantly above the regression line, while Label B points to a trough where the price is significantly below the regression line, illustrating price dispersion and deviation from the central trend.
knowledge_claims: A linear regression line represents the mathematical central tendency of a price series over a specific period.
Prices frequently deviate above (Point A) and below (Point B) the linear regression line rather than clinging tightly to it.
The distance of price bars from the linear regression line indicates the level of price dispersion and volatility.

fig_p260_051.png.title: Linear Regression Trendlines on a V-Shaped Price Series
fig_p260_051.png.description: An OHLC bar chart illustrating how the choice of start and end points affects linear regression trendlines. A single thick blue line represents a linear regression calculated across the entire V-shaped dataset, resulting in a nearly flat line that fails to capture either trend. In contrast, two thinner blue lines represent separate linear regressions calculated for the downward and upward phases of the V-shape, accurately reflecting the distinct trends.
knowledge_claims: A single linear regression line drawn across a V-shaped price series results in a nearly horizontal line that fails to represent either the downward or upward trend.
Segmenting the price series into distinct downward and upward phases allows linear regression lines to accurately capture and forecast the individual trends.
The slope and validity of a linear regression trendline are highly dependent on the chosen start and end points of the data series.

fig_p266_052.png.title: Model Channel Construction and Validation
fig_p266_052.png.description: A conceptual diagram illustrating how to construct and validate a price channel in technical analysis. It shows a series of price bars where a support line is first established by connecting two lows. A parallel resistance line is then projected starting from a relative high. The extended lines are validated as actual support and resistance when subsequent price peaks and troughs touch but do not break these lines, as highlighted by circled areas.
knowledge_claims: A price channel is initiated by connecting two lows to form a support line and projecting a parallel resistance line from a relative high.
Extended trendlines remain hypothetical until subsequent price action touches them without breaking through.
Validation of the channel occurs when future price highs and lows touch the extended resistance and support lines, confirming their significance.

fig_p266_053_vec.png.title: Why Are the Lines Parallel?
fig_p266_053_vec.png.description: A text callout box with a light blue background explaining the relationship between support and resistance lines in technical analysis. It describes how drawing a support line connecting a series of lows often reveals a parallel resistance line connecting the highest highs, leading charting software to include standard commands for creating parallel lines.
knowledge_claims: Connecting a series of lows with a support line often reveals a parallel resistance line that connects the highest highs.
The phenomenon of parallel support and resistance lines is common enough that most charting software programs feature a standard command to 'create parallel line'.

fig_p267_054_vec.png.title: Explanations for Parallel Support and Resistance Lines
fig_p267_054_vec.png.description: A text-based informational block explaining three theories on why support and resistance lines often appear parallel, forming trading channels. The theories attribute this phenomenon to orderly trading crowd behavior, technical analysts' reliance on cyclical price patterns, and the human psychological tendency to impose order and patterns on market data.
knowledge_claims: Channel orderliness may arise from market participants collectively recognizing and acting on relatively high (resistance) and relatively cheap (support) price levels.
Technical analysts often design trading plans around the expectation that prices will cyclically alternate between parallel support and resistance lines.
The appearance of parallel channels is reinforced by the human innate tendency to impose order and patterns, though these channels are not inevitable or guaranteed to persist indefinitely.

fig_p271_055.png.title: Linear Regression Channel Extension and Breakout
fig_p271_055.png.description: A daily stock price chart spanning from September to May (2002-2003) illustrating the application of linear regression channels. The chart shows two sets of parallel channel lines tracking an uptrend. An annotation points to 'EXTENSION STARTS HERE' where a shorter channel's boundaries are projected forward, and a circled region highlights where the 'CHANNEL BROKEN TO THE UPSIDE' occurs as the price breaks above the upper channel line.
knowledge_claims: A linear regression channel can be extended forward (projected) to anticipate future support and resistance zones.
A price breaking above the upper boundary of a regression channel indicates a strong bullish breakout to the upside.
Multiple channels of varying lengths and widths can be drawn on the same chart to analyze short-term versus long-term trend dynamics.

fig_p276_056.png.title: False Breakout in an Ascending Channel
fig_p276_056.png.description: This diagram illustrates an ascending price channel defined by two parallel upward-sloping trendlines representing support and resistance. Inside the channel, vertical bars represent price action. An arrow points to a single vertical bar that briefly penetrates below the lower support line before the price returns to the channel, labeled as a 'FALSE BREAKOUT'.
knowledge_claims: A false breakout occurs when a price temporarily breaches a support or resistance level but fails to sustain the momentum, quickly returning within the established trendlines.
In an ascending channel, a false breakout to the downside temporarily penetrates the lower support line before the upward trend resumes.
Not all breaches of trendlines signify a true trend reversal or breakout; some are temporary market noise or manipulation.

fig_p280_057.png.title: Comparison of Price Breakouts in Orderly vs. Disorderly Channels
fig_p280_057.png.description: The image contains two diagrams illustrating price breakouts from upward-sloping trend channels. The left diagram shows an orderly channel with low-volatility price bars neatly contained within the channel boundaries before a clear downward breakout. The right diagram shows a disorderly channel with high-volatility, large price bars where a breakout of the same size is less distinct and harder to verify as authentic.
knowledge_claims: A price breakout from an orderly, low-volatility channel is more reliable and easier to identify than one from a disorderly channel.
In a disorderly channel characterized by high volatility and large price bars, a breakout bar of the same absolute size as an orderly breakout may not be meaningful or authentic.
Orderliness in trading channels is defined by low price volatility and neat alignment within channel boundaries.

fig_p282_058.png.title: Blowout Top Breakout in an Ascending Channel
fig_p282_058.png.description: This diagram illustrates a blowout top breakout within an ascending price channel. Price bars initially move orderly within the parallel trendlines, but then accelerate sharply upward, breaking above the upper channel line. This rapid, high-volatility surge (the blowout top) is short-lived and is immediately followed by a steep decline where the price falls back through the channel and breaks below the lower trendline.
knowledge_claims: A blowout top breakout occurs when prices accelerate rapidly and break above the upper boundary of an existing ascending channel.
An upside breakout in an uptrend can signal extreme market enthusiasm or a blowoff phase that often precedes a sharp trend reversal.
Following a blowout top, prices frequently collapse back through the channel, breaking the lower support line.

fig_p285_059.png.title: Pivot Point and Support/Resistance Level Formulas
fig_p285_059.png.description: This image presents the mathematical formulas used to calculate the standard Pivot Point and its associated support and resistance levels. It defines the Pivot Point as the average of the High, Close, and Low prices, and details the step-by-step calculations for three levels of resistance (R1, R2, R3) and three levels of support (S1, S2, S3) using previous high, low, and calculated values.
knowledge_claims: The Pivot Point is calculated as the sum of the High, Close, and Low prices divided by 3.
Resistance Level 1 (R1) is calculated by multiplying the Pivot Point by 2 and subtracting the Previous Low.
Support Level 1 (S1) is calculated by multiplying the Pivot Point by 2 and subtracting the Previous High.
Subsequent support and resistance levels (R2, R3, S2, S3) are calculated recursively using the primary Pivot Point and the first-level support/resistance values.

fig_p286_060.png.title: Pivot Point Support and Resistance Levels on a Price Chart
fig_p286_060.png.description: A daily bar chart of a financial asset from April to November showing price action with technical analysis overlays. It features a downward-sloping price channel in May and June, followed by a recovery. Horizontal lines mark three resistance levels (R1, R2, R3) between 1110 and 1130, and three support levels (S1, S2, S3) between 1040 and 1060. An ascending trendline connects the lows from July through September, indicating a rising support zone.
knowledge_claims: Resistance levels (R1, R2, R3) represent price zones where selling pressure historically overcomes buying pressure, halting upward movement.
Support levels (S1, S2, S3) represent price zones where buying pressure historically overcomes selling pressure, halting downward movement.
The intersection of horizontal support levels with an ascending trendline creates a stronger confluence zone of support.
A breakout above established resistance levels (like R1, R2, R3) or below support levels (S1, S2, S3) signals a potential trend continuation or reversal.

fig_p287_061.png.title: Trendline Channels and Support/Resistance Levels on a Stock Chart
fig_p287_061.png.description: A daily stock price chart spanning from April to November showing technical analysis patterns. The first half of the chart displays a downward-sloping price channel defined by parallel blue lines. In July, the price hits a low and transitions into an upward-sloping channel marked by thicker blue lines. Horizontal grey lines indicate key support and resistance zones, while two black circles highlight critical pivot points where price action interacts with major trendlines.
knowledge_claims: The chart demonstrates a transition from a downward-sloping channel to an upward-sloping channel, indicating a trend reversal.
Horizontal grey lines mark key historical support and resistance levels where price consolidation occurs.
Circled areas highlight critical intersections of price action and trendlines, representing key pivot points for traders.

fig_p293_062.png.title: Moving Average Trend Following and Outlier Identification
fig_p293_062.png.description: A stock price chart spanning from late August to mid-November, showing daily price bars plotted against a thick black moving average line. Upward-pointing arrows indicate upward trend reversals or buy signals, while a downward-pointing arrow indicates a downward trend reversal. Circled areas highlight price peaks and troughs where the price deviates from the moving average, and a grey arrow labeled 'OUTLIER' points to a single anomalous price bar in mid-September.
knowledge_claims: Moving averages smooth price data to help traders identify and measure the direction of a trend.
Arrows on the chart indicate key crossover or trend reversal points that traders use as buy or sell signals.
An outlier represents a temporary price movement that deviates from the established moving average trend line.
Circled areas demonstrate how price extremes (peaks and troughs) lead the lagging moving average line before a trend reversal is confirmed.

fig_p301_063.png.title: Trend Tidiness and Noise Levels in Moving Averages
fig_p301_063.png.description: This diagram illustrates three distinct market trend scenarios relative to a central trend line representing a moving average. The top panel shows a 'Sideways Trend - Low Noise' with price bars fluctuating tightly around a flat horizontal line. The middle panel shows a 'Tidy Trend - Low Noise' where price bars closely track an upward-sloping trend line with minimal deviation. The bottom panel shows an 'Untidy Trend - High Noise' where price bars exhibit large, erratic vertical deviations and outliers far from the upward-sloping trend line.
knowledge_claims: A sideways trend is characterized by horizontal price movement with low noise, which typically defeats trend-following strategies.
A tidy trend features low noise where price fluctuations remain close to the moving average line.
An untidy trend features high noise with significant price deviations and outliers far from the moving average, increasing trading difficulty.

fig_p310_064.png.title: Comparison of 10-Day Moving Average Types
fig_p310_064.png.description: A stock price chart from March to July displaying daily price bars alongside four different 10-day moving averages: Simple (thin red line), Weighted (thick red line), Exponential (black line), and Adaptive (thick blue line). The y-axis on the right represents price levels from 72 to 91, while the x-axis shows weekly intervals across five months.
knowledge_claims: The Adaptive Moving Average (thick blue line) is the smoothest of the four types, effectively filtering out short-term price spikes and noise.
The Weighted Moving Average (thick red line) and Exponential Moving Average (black line) react more quickly to recent price changes compared to the Simple Moving Average.
Different mathematical formulations of moving averages result in varying degrees of lag and sensitivity to price trends.

fig_p312_065.png.title: Two moving average crossover model
fig_p312_065.png.description: A stock price chart from August to November showing daily price bars plotted with two moving averages: a fast-moving red line (5-day) and a slower-moving black line (20-day). The y-axis on the right represents price values from 55 to 85. Three black arrows point to crossover events: two upward-pointing arrows (early August and mid-October) indicating buy signals when the red line crosses above the black line, and one downward-pointing arrow (early September) indicating a sell signal when the red line crosses below the black line.
knowledge_claims: A buy signal is triggered when the shorter-term moving average (red line) crosses above the longer-term moving average (black line).
A sell signal is triggered when the shorter-term moving average (red line) crosses below the longer-term moving average (black line).
Using a dual moving average crossover system helps filter out short-term price fluctuations and establishes clearer trend-following entry and exit points.

fig_p315_066.png.title: Stock Chart with Three Moving Averages
fig_p315_066.png.description: A daily stock price chart from late April to mid-July showing price bars plotted with three moving averages: a 5-day (red line), a 10-day (blue line), and a 20-day (thick black line). Three arrows indicate key crossover signals: an upward arrow around April 21 marking a buy signal, a downward arrow around May 19 marking a sell signal, and another upward arrow around July 7.
knowledge_claims: A buy signal is confirmed when both the 5-day and 10-day moving averages cross above the 20-day moving average, as indicated by the upward arrows on April 21 and July 7.
A sell signal is triggered when the short-term (5-day) moving average crosses below the longer-term moving averages, as shown by the downward arrow on May 19.
Using three moving averages instead of two helps traders avoid false signals and whipsaw losses during sideways or choppy market periods.

fig_p316_067.png.title: Moving Average Ribbon Convergence and Divergence
fig_p316_067.png.description: A candlestick stock chart overlaid with a moving average ribbon consisting of multiple red lines representing different timeframes. The chart is divided by vertical blue lines to highlight phases of convergence and divergence. Arrows point to areas of 'Convergence' where the ribbon lines contract tightly together, indicating a fading trend or potential reversal, and 'Divergence' where the ribbon lines fan out widely, indicating a strong and robust trend.
knowledge_claims: A moving average ribbon is formed by plotting multiple moving averages of different periods on a single chart.
Divergence in a moving average ribbon occurs when the shorter-term averages outrun the longer-term ones, causing the ribbon to widen and indicating a strong trend.
Convergence occurs when the moving averages contract toward a single line, indicating that trendedness is fading or a reversal is imminent.

fig_p319_068.png.title: Moving Average Convergence and Divergence Chart
fig_p319_068.png.description: A stock price chart spanning from late April to early July, showing two moving averages in the top panel (a thin red line and a thick red line) plotted against price bars. The bottom panel displays the convergence-divergence indicator line (red) relative to a zero line (blue). An arrow labeled 'DIVERGENCE' points to a peak in the indicator where the two moving averages are furthest apart, while an arrow labeled 'CONVERGENCE' points to a declining phase where the moving averages are drawing closer together.
knowledge_claims: Divergence occurs when the distance between a short-term and a long-term moving average increases, represented by a rising indicator line.
Convergence occurs when the distance between a short-term and a long-term moving average decreases, represented by a falling indicator line.
The convergence-divergence indicator is calculated by subtracting the long-term moving average from the short-term moving average, oscillating around a zero line.

fig_p320_069.png.title: Convergence and Divergence of Moving Averages Chart
fig_p320_069.png.description: A technical stock chart showing price bars with two exponential moving averages (12-day and 26-day) in the top panel, and their difference (the convergence-divergence indicator) in the bottom panel. The bottom panel plots the indicator against a blue zero line, with a dotted signal line. Black arrows highlight key crossover points: an upward arrow in mid-April shows a bullish crossover where the indicator crosses above zero, a downward arrow in mid-May shows a peak divergence, a downward arrow in early June shows a bearish crossover below zero, and an upward arrow in late June/early July shows a potential bullish turn.
knowledge_claims: The convergence-divergence indicator line is calculated by subtracting the longer-term (26-day) moving average from the shorter-term (12-day) moving average.
When the two moving averages cross on the price chart, the convergence-divergence indicator line crosses the zero line in the indicator window.
An upward trend in the indicator line represents divergence where the shorter-term average is rising faster than the longer-term average, while a downward trend represents convergence.

fig_p321_070.png.title: MACD Histogram Divergence Chart
fig_p321_070.png.description: A stock price chart from April to July showing a comparison between price action and the MACD histogram. The top panel displays daily price bars with two moving averages, where an upward grey arrow highlights rising prices in mid-to-late May. The bottom panel displays the corresponding MACD histogram with red bars relative to a zero line, where a downward grey arrow highlights decreasing momentum during the same period, illustrating a bearish divergence.
knowledge_claims: The chart demonstrates a bearish divergence where the stock price continues to rise (indicated by the upward arrow) while the MACD histogram peaks and begins to decline (indicated by the downward arrow).
The MACD histogram represents the difference between the MACD line and its signal line, plotted as vertical bars above and below a zero line.
Divergence between price action and MACD histogram momentum can serve as an early warning sign of an impending trend reversal or price correction.

fig_p328_071.png.title: Momentum Indicator Leading Price Action
fig_p328_071.png.description: A two-panel financial chart comparing a momentum indicator (top) with a daily price chart (bottom). The top panel shows a red momentum line plotted against a baseline of 100, highlighting a crossover below 100 that occurs one day before a major opening gap down in the price chart. It also shows the momentum line crossing back above 100 while the price is still in a downward trend, illustrating how momentum can lead price action.
knowledge_claims: The momentum indicator crossed below the 100 baseline exactly one day prior to a significant opening gap down in the stock price.
The momentum indicator turned positive (crossed above 100) while the underlying stock price was still falling, signaling a potential shift in trend strength before the price bottomed out.
A momentum value below 100 indicates that the current price is lower than the price from the lookback period.

fig_p328_072_vec.png.title: The Difference Between Momentum and Momentum Investing
fig_p328_072_vec.png.description: An informational text box explaining the distinction between technical momentum (an arithmetic calculation of trend speed) and momentum investing (an undisciplined strategy of buying rising assets without systematic entry/exit criteria).
knowledge_claims: Momentum is a precise arithmetic calculation used by technical traders to measure the speed or rate of change of a trend.
Momentum investing is an undisciplined strategy based on buying a security simply because its price is rising, without systematic entry or exit criteria.
Unlike technical momentum analysis, momentum investing lacks measurement of the rate of change or consideration of other systematic factors.

fig_p335_073.png.title: Momentum and Price Divergence
fig_p335_073.png.description: A two-panel chart illustrating the concept of divergence between price and momentum. The top panel displays a red momentum indicator line fluctuating around a blue horizontal zero line, with a grey arrow pointing downwards to show decreasing momentum. The bottom panel displays a black price bar chart with a grey arrow pointing upwards to show an increasing price trend. This visual contrast demonstrates a bearish divergence where price rises while momentum falls.
knowledge_claims: Divergence occurs when the direction of the momentum indicator moves opposite to the direction of the price trend.
A rising price trend accompanied by a falling momentum indicator indicates that the upward price movement is losing speed or strength.
The momentum indicator measures the rate of price change rather than the absolute price level, allowing it to decline even while prices are still rising.

fig_p339_074.png.title: Comparison of RSI, Momentum Indicators, and Price Action
fig_p339_074.png.description: A three-panel financial chart showing the relationship between the Relative Strength Index (RSI), momentum indicators, and price action over a four-month period from August to November. The top panel displays the RSI oscillator with horizontal threshold lines at 70 (Overbought) and 30 (Oversold). The middle panel shows raw and smoothed momentum indicators centered around a baseline of 100. The bottom panel displays the underlying security's price bars with hand-drawn gray trendlines highlighting a major downward trend followed by a sharp recovery.
knowledge_claims: The RSI indicator identifies overbought conditions when it rises above the 70 threshold and oversold conditions when it falls below the 30 threshold.
A price bottom in mid-October corresponds directly with the RSI dipping below the 30 oversold line and momentum indicators reaching their lowest levels.
The momentum indicators in the middle panel fluctuate around a baseline value of 100, mirroring the broader directional shifts of the price trend.

fig_p344_075.png.title: Stochastic Oscillator with Overbought and Oversold Levels
fig_p344_075.png.description: A technical analysis chart showing a stock's price movement in the bottom panel and its corresponding Stochastic Oscillator in the top panel from August to November. The oscillator features two lines (red and black) representing %K and %D, with horizontal boundary lines at 80 (Overbought) and 20 (Oversold). A black oval highlights a period in October where the oscillator remains sustained in the overbought region above 80 while the stock price experiences a sharp upward gap and rally.
knowledge_claims: The Stochastic Oscillator uses a scale from 0 to 100, where values above 80 indicate an overbought condition and values below 20 indicate an oversold condition.
A sustained period where both the %K (red) and %D (black) lines remain above the 80 threshold (highlighted by the oval) corresponds to a strong upward price momentum or rally.
The bottom panel displays price bars that correlate directly with the oscillator's peaks and troughs, showing how oversold conditions (below 20) align with price bottoms in early October.

fig_p346_076.png.title: Stochastic Oscillator and Price Chart Showing Bullish Divergence
fig_p346_076.png.description: A dual-panel financial chart showing a stochastic oscillator in the top panel and corresponding price bars in the bottom panel over February and March. The top panel plots the %K (red) and %D (black) lines with horizontal threshold lines at 80 (overbought) and 20 (oversold). It highlights three successive higher peaks (labeled 1, 2, and 3) and an ascending support line drawn under the oscillator's troughs, illustrating a bullish divergence pattern relative to the price action below.
knowledge_claims: The stochastic oscillator uses two lines, %K (fast) and %D (slow, 3-day moving average of %K), to identify momentum and potential trend reversals.
Horizontal lines at 80 and 20 represent standard overbought and oversold thresholds respectively.

A series of higher lows or 'knees' in the stochastic oscillator can be connected with an ascending support line to indicate underlying strength (bullish divergence) even when price action is relatively flat or declining.
Crossovers where %K crosses %D can signal buy or sell opportunities, especially near the overbought/oversold boundaries.

fig_p348_077.png.title: Stochastic Oscillator in a Strongly Trending Market
fig_p348_077.png.description: A financial chart displaying a price bar chart in the lower panel and a stochastic oscillator in the upper panel. The price chart shows a strong, continuous downtrend from June to late July, marked by a blue descending trendline. The stochastic oscillator in the upper panel remains trapped below the 20 level (oversold boundary) for an extended period, highlighted by a black oval, while the price continues to decline.
knowledge_claims: In a strongly trending market, the stochastic oscillator can remain in the oversold region (below 20) for a prolonged period without a price reversal.
Relying on oversold stochastic signals to buy during a strong downtrend can result in premature entries and significant losses.
The indicator lacks trend identification capability, making it dangerous to use without considering the overall market trend direction.

fig_p352_078.png.title: Market States of Variance and Trending
fig_p352_078.png.description: A conceptual price bar chart illustrating different market conditions based on the relationship between variance (volatility/bar size) and trending behavior. It labels five distinct phases: low variance with trending, low variance without trending, contraction of bar high-low, high variance with trending, and high variance without trending.
knowledge_claims: Market behavior can be categorized into distinct states by combining the degree of price variance (bar height) with the presence or absence of a directional trend.
Low variance with trending features small, consistent price bars moving steadily in a single direction.
High variance without trending is characterized by large price bars that fluctuate widely without establishing a clear directional trend.
A contraction of the bar high-low range represents a period of extremely low volatility that often precedes a transition to a high-variance trending phase.

fig_p356_079.png.title: Maximum Move Volatility Indicator Comparison
fig_p356_079.png.description: A three-panel financial chart showing a rolling 30-day maximum move indicator (top, red line), the underlying stock price (middle, black line), and trading volume (bottom, blue bars) over an 18-month period. The top panel shows volatility decreasing from a peak of over 30 to under 10, highlighted by a downward-pointing grey arrow. Conversely, the middle panel shows the stock price stabilizing and slightly rising from late 2002 to mid-2003, highlighted by a slightly upward-sloping grey arrow.
knowledge_claims: The maximum move indicator (highest high minus lowest low over a rolling 30-day period) measures historical price volatility.
A decreasing maximum move indicator signifies a contraction in price volatility, even if the underlying stock price is in a mild uptrend.
Lower volatility (as shown by the declining red line in the latter half of the chart) reduces the risk of large price swings but also limits short-term profit potential.

fig_p358_080.png.title: Orderly vs. Disorderly (Noisy) Price Trends
fig_p358_080.png.description: The image contains two conceptual line charts comparing price movements from point A to point B. The top chart, labeled 'ORDERLY', shows a relatively smooth, low-volatility upward trend with small, consistent fluctuations around a central linear regression line. The bottom chart, labeled 'DISORDERLY (NOISY)', shows a highly volatile upward trend with large, erratic swings above and below the same central linear regression line, despite ending at the same net change point B.
knowledge_claims: Both orderly and disorderly price series can achieve the exact same net change from point A to point B.
A disorderly (noisy) price series exhibits much higher volatility and larger fluctuations around the trend line than an orderly series.
Disorderly price series imply a greater risk of loss if an investor is forced to exit the position before the period ends.

fig_p360_081.png.title: Average True Range (ATR) Indicator and Price Divergence
fig_p360_081.png.description: A two-panel financial chart illustrating the relationship between price action and volatility using the Average True Range (ATR) indicator. The top panel shows a red line representing a declining ATR, indicating contracting volatility. The bottom panel shows price bars forming a rising support line and reaching a higher high, followed by a range contraction (wedge pattern) and a subsequent downward breakout below the support line.
knowledge_claims: A declining Average True Range (ATR) indicates contracting volatility even as prices are rising.
A divergence between rising prices (making higher highs) and falling ATR serves as a warning sign of an impending trend change or breakout.
Range contraction often precedes a breakout, where the price breaks through established support or resistance lines.

fig_p361_082.png.title: Bollinger Bands Chart Patterns
fig_p361_082.png.description: A stock price candlestick chart overlaid with red Bollinger Bands consisting of an upper band, a middle moving average line, and a lower band. The chart highlights key technical analysis concepts: 'WALKING UP THE BAND' during a strong uptrend where price candles ride the upper band, and 'THE SQUEEZE' where the bands constrict closely together, indicating a period of low volatility before a trend change.
knowledge_claims: Walking up the band occurs when the price continuously touches or rides the upper Bollinger Band, signaling a strong upward trend continuation.
The Squeeze is characterized by the narrowing of the upper and lower bands, indicating a period of low volatility that often precedes a sharp price movement.
Circles on the chart highlight key areas where the price interacts with the middle moving average line or fails to break back above it during a downtrend.

fig_p364_083.png.title: Average True Range Band and Trendline Breakout
fig_p364_083.png.description: This stock chart illustrates the use of Average True Range (ATR) bands to identify true trend reversals. It shows price bars moving in an uptrend along a blue support line, bounded by red upper and lower ATR bands around a central grey moving average. At the far right, a black circle highlights a sharp downward price bar that breaks both the blue support trendline and the widened lower red ATR band, signaling a confirmed trend reversal.
knowledge_claims: Widening the lower ATR band during an uptrend helps distinguish normal corrective retracements from actual trend reversals.
A valid trend reversal is confirmed when the price breaks both the established support trendline and the widened lower ATR band simultaneously.
The central grey line represents a moving average of the median price, while the red bands are plotted by adding and subtracting a multiple of the ATR.

fig_p373_084.png.title: Point and Figure (P&F) Chart Reversal Example
fig_p373_084.png.description: A simplified Point and Figure (P&F) chart grid illustrating a price reversal. The first column shows a price rise from $9 to $13 represented by a column of five 'X' marks. The second column shows a reversal to a new low that is $3 below the previous high ($13), starting with an 'O' at the $10 level and a question mark at the $9 level, demonstrating how a 3-box reversal is plotted.
knowledge_claims: A column of 'X's represents a continuous upward price movement, with each 'X' corresponding to a specific price increment.
A reversal to a new column of 'O's occurs when the price drops by a specified threshold (such as $3 or 3 boxes) below the previous high.
The first 'O' in a reversal column is plotted at the reversal price level (in this case, $10, which is $3 below the high of $13).

fig_p379_085.png.title: Point and Figure Chart Patterns
fig_p379_085.png.description: This diagram illustrates four common patterns in Point and Figure (P&F) technical analysis charting. The top-left panel shows a 'Break of Horizontal Resistance' where a column of Xs breaks above a horizontal resistance line. The top-right panel shows 'Conventional Support' with an upward-sloping trendline supporting columns of Xs and Os. The bottom-left panel depicts a 'Double Top Reversal' where two columns of Xs fail to break resistance and are followed by a downward column of Os. The bottom-right panel shows a 'Double Top Bullish Continuation' where a column of Xs successfully breaks above a horizontal resistance line established by previous peaks.
