knowledge_claims: A Break of Horizontal Resistance is identified when a column of Xs rises above a horizontal line defined by previous peaks.
Conventional Support in P&F charting is represented by an upward-sloping trendline drawn below the lows of the columns.
A Double Top Reversal occurs when the price fails to break a horizontal resistance level twice and subsequently reverses downward in a column of Os.
A Double Top Bullish Continuation pattern is confirmed when a column of Xs breaks above a horizontal resistance line formed by multiple previous peaks.

fig_p383_086.png.title: Point and Figure Vertical Projection Method
fig_p383_086.png.description: This diagram illustrates the vertical projection method used in Point and Figure (P&F) charting to calculate a target price. It shows columns of 'X's and 'O's, highlighting a starting low (a circled 'X' at the bottom of a column) and tracing an arrow to the top of a subsequent column of 'X's. The formula on the right states: 'NO. BOXES IN REVERSAL COLUMN × REVERSAL AMOUNT + STARTING LOW = TARGET PRICE'.
knowledge_claims: The target price in a vertical P&F projection is calculated by multiplying the number of boxes in the reversal column by the reversal amount and adding the starting low.
The starting low is defined as the lowest 'X' in the initial column of the upward move.
The vertical projection method uses the height of a specific column to estimate the potential magnitude of a price move.

fig_p385_087.png.title: Point and Figure Horizontal Projection Method
fig_p385_087.png.description: A diagram illustrating how to calculate a target price using the horizontal projection method on a Point and Figure (P&F) chart. It shows a sequence of 'X' and 'O' columns with a horizontal bracket indicating the width of the base columns, a circled 'X' representing the starting low, and the formula: NO. OF BASE COLUMNS x REVERSAL AMOUNT + STARTING LOW = TARGET PRICE.
knowledge_claims: The target price is calculated by multiplying the number of base columns by the reversal amount and adding the starting low.
The base width is measured horizontally across the consolidation area before a breakout occurs.
The starting low is identified as the lowest point (often circled) within the established base columns.

fig_p392_088.png.title: Moving Average Crossover and Double Top Confirmation Chart
fig_p392_088.png.description: A technical analysis stock chart showing price bars plotted against a vertical price axis ranging from 12.10 to 13.90. The chart illustrates a double top pattern marked by two arrow symbols at the peaks, a horizontal 'Double Top Confirmation Line' acting as support, and a 'Moving Average Crossover Sell Signal' where the faster moving average line crosses below the slower, thicker moving average line.
knowledge_claims: A double top pattern is identified by two consecutive price peaks of approximately equal height, signaling a potential bearish trend reversal.
The double top confirmation line acts as a critical support level that, when broken, confirms the bearish pattern.
A bearish moving average crossover occurs when a shorter-term moving average crosses below a longer-term moving average, serving as a sell signal.

fig_p394_089.png.title: Confirming Indicators in a Trading System
fig_p394_089.png.description: A three-pane stock chart illustrating the combination of primary trend-following indicators with early entry and exit indicators. The middle pane shows price bars with a 5-day moving average (thin blue line) and a 20-day moving average (thick red line), highlighting the 'Primary Entry Indicator' at their crossover and the 'Primary Exit Indicator' when price drops below the 5-day average. The bottom pane displays an 'Early Entry Indicator' using a momentum line crossing a threshold, while the top pane displays an 'Early Exit Indicator' using an overbought sentiment line crossing below a threshold.
knowledge_claims: A primary entry signal is triggered when a shorter-term moving average crosses above a longer-term moving average.
An early entry indicator, such as a momentum oscillator, can identify entry points several days before a moving average crossover occurs.
A primary exit signal is triggered when the price closes below the shorter-term moving average.
An early exit indicator, such as an overbought sentiment oscillator, can signal a sell opportunity before the price crosses below the moving average, preserving more profit.

fig_p396_090.png.title: Technical Analysis Chart with Multiple Indicators and Signals
fig_p396_090.png.description: A multi-pane financial chart illustrating the combination of several technical indicators. The top three panels display the Stochastic Oscillator, Momentum, and MACD indicators. The main bottom panel shows a price bar chart with moving averages, a downward-sloping channel, a 'Moving Average Crossover Sell Signal' marked by an arrow, and a 'Trailing Stop Loss Is Hit At This Bar' label pointing to a specific price bar near the end of the trend.
knowledge_claims: A moving average crossover can be used to generate a sell signal during a downward trend.
Multiple indicators like Stochastic Oscillator, Momentum, and MACD can be plotted simultaneously to confirm trend direction and momentum.
A trailing stop loss can protect profits or limit losses by triggering an exit when the price crosses a predetermined threshold.

fig_p399_091.png.title: Price Channel with Relative Strength Index (RSI) Confirmation
fig_p399_091.png.description: A financial candlestick chart showing a downward-sloping price channel with three parallel blue lines representing the upper, middle, and lower boundaries. Above the price chart, a Relative Strength Index (RSI) panel displays a red line oscillating between 20 and 75. The chart illustrates how RSI peaks and troughs align with price touches at the channel boundaries to identify wave turning points.
knowledge_claims: Prices oscillating within a downward-sloping standard error channel exhibit wave-like behavior.
The Relative Strength Index (RSI) can be used to confirm potential tops and bottoms when prices reach the channel boundaries.
Troughs in the RSI line correspond to price touches near the lower channel boundary, indicating potential buying opportunities or trend exhaustion.
Peaks in the RSI line correspond to price touches near the upper channel boundary, indicating potential selling opportunities.

fig_p410_092.png.title: Sine Wave Model of a Market Cycle
fig_p410_092.png.description: A line chart showing a series of blue data points connected by a line that forms a regular, repeating wave pattern resembling a sine wave. The chart has horizontal gridlines but lacks axis labels or numerical values, illustrating the theoretical, symmetrical nature of a cycle with peaks and troughs.
knowledge_claims: A market cycle is modeled on the symmetrical, repeating pattern of a sine wave.
The wave pattern consists of alternating peaks (highs) and troughs (lows) occurring at regular intervals.
In technical analysis, cycles are used to represent regular and repetitive price movements over time.

fig_p415_093.png.title: The Wyckoff Model of Market Cycles
fig_p415_093.png.description: A conceptual line diagram illustrating market cycles through a sinusoidal wave. The wave represents price fluctuations driven by supply and demand dynamics. The trough is labeled 'Oversold—Supply Exhausted', the rising phase is labeled 'Demand Rising', the peak is labeled 'Overbought—Demand Exhausted', and the falling phase is labeled 'Supply > Demand'.
knowledge_claims: The lowest point of a market cycle (trough) represents an 'Oversold' condition where supply is exhausted.
An upward price movement is characterized by rising demand exceeding supply.
The highest point of a market cycle (peak) represents an 'Overbought' condition where demand is exhausted.
A downward price movement occurs when supply exceeds demand.

fig_p426_094.png.title: Gann 50 Percent Retracement Rule
fig_p426_094.png.description: A conceptual line diagram illustrating W.D. Gann's 50 percent retracement rule. The price line starts at $10 and rises to $30, representing a $20 upward move. It then retraces downward to $20, which is exactly 50% of the initial $20 gain. This $20 level is labeled '50% RETRACEMENT BUY HERE', followed by a dashed line projecting a subsequent upward price movement.
knowledge_claims: A 50% retracement occurs when a security's price declines by half of its previous upward move (e.g., falling from $30 to $20 after rising from $10 to $30).
The 50% retracement level is identified as a high-probability buy zone ('BUY HERE') where the downward correction is expected to end.
Following a successful 50% retracement, the price is projected to resume its original upward trend.

fig_p429_095.png.title: Wavelike Trend and Retracement Percentages
fig_p429_095.png.description: A stock price chart illustrating the wavelike nature of an uptrend, featuring three distinct upward impulse waves (indicated by blue lines) and three downward corrective retracements (indicated by thick black lines). The y-axis represents price levels ranging from 1.35 to 1.65, and the x-axis shows monthly intervals over a multi-year period. The chart labels three specific retracements with their respective percentage pullbacks: Retracement 1 at 69.2%, Retracement 2 at 35.7%, and Retracement 3 at 78%.
knowledge_claims: Financial market trends progress in a wavelike pattern consisting of alternating impulse moves in the direction of the trend and corrective retracements against it.
The depth of corrective retracements can vary significantly within the same overall trend, as demonstrated by retracement values of 35.7%, 69.2%, and 78%.
An overall upward trend is maintained even when individual corrective retracements pull back a substantial portion of the preceding upward move.

fig_p433_096_vec.png.title: Translating Ichimoku Kinko Hyo
fig_p433_096_vec.png.description: An informational text box explaining the origin, history, and translation of the Japanese technical analysis tool 'Ichimoku Kinko Hyo'. It details the linguistic meaning of each word in the term, translating to 'one look equilibrium chart' or 'at a glance balance chart', and attributes its creation to Goichi Hosoda in 1969.
knowledge_claims: Goichi Hosoda first described Ichimoku Kinko Hyo in 1969 based on 1930s trader techniques.
The literal translation of 'Ichimoku' is 'glance' or 'at a glance'.
The literal translation of 'Kinko' is 'balance' or 'equilibrium'.
The literal translation of 'Hyo' is 'bar chart'.
The core concepts of Ichimoku to keep in mind are 'balance or equilibrium' and 'at a glance'.

fig_p438_097.png.title: Ichimoku Indicator: Tenkan and Kijun Lines
fig_p438_097.png.description: A candlestick price chart illustrating two primary components of the Ichimoku Kinko Hyo indicator: the Tenkan-sen (represented by a light gray line) and the Kijun-sen (represented by a thick black line). The Tenkan line closely tracks short-term price movements, while the Kijun line is slower, showing flat plateau-like segments during periods of price consolidation.
knowledge_claims: The Tenkan line (light gray) reacts faster to price changes and closely hugs the candlestick price action.
The Kijun line (black) reacts slower to price changes, representing a longer-term equilibrium level that often flattens out during consolidation.
The relative positioning and crossovers of the Tenkan and Kijun lines are used by traders to determine trend direction and momentum.

fig_p438_098.png.title: Ichimoku Cloud Boundaries (Senkou Span A and Senkou Span B)
fig_p438_098.png.description: A financial candlestick chart illustrating the components of the Ichimoku Cloud (Kumo). Two callout boxes identify 'Senkou Span A' as the Upper Cloud Boundary and 'Senkou Span B' as the Lower Cloud Boundary. The shaded areas (clouds) represent dynamic support and resistance zones relative to the price action.
knowledge_claims: Senkou Span A serves as the upper boundary of the Ichimoku cloud (Kumo) under certain market conditions.
Senkou Span B serves as the lower boundary of the Ichimoku cloud (Kumo).
The space between Senkou Span A and Senkou Span B forms the 'cloud' which acts as a visual representation of support and resistance.

fig_p439_099.png.title: Ichimoku Chart with Chikou Span Highlighted
fig_p439_099.png.description: A financial candlestick chart illustrating the Ichimoku Kinko Hyo indicator system. A prominent solid black line is labeled 'Chikou (Black)' with an arrow pointing to it, representing the lagging span. The chart also displays the Kumo (cloud) in light and dark gray shades, which act as support and resistance zones relative to the price candlesticks.
knowledge_claims: The Chikou Span (black line) represents the current closing price plotted backwards in time.
The Kumo (cloud) shifts from light gray (support) to dark gray (resistance) as the market trend changes from bullish to bearish.
When prices remain significantly below the dark gray cloud, it indicates a sustained bearish trend where the cloud acts as a strong resistance barrier.

fig_p439_100_vec.png.title: Ichimoku's Embedded Momentum and the 26-Day Parameter
fig_p439_100_vec.png.description: An informational text box explaining the historical and mathematical significance of the parameters used in Ichimoku moving averages, specifically focusing on the 9, 26, and 52 period settings. It highlights why the 26-day parameter is used, its origin in 1960s Japanese six-day trading weeks, and its independent validation by Gerald Appel for the MACD indicator.
knowledge_claims: Ichimoku uses three specific periods for its moving averages: 9, 26, and 52 periods.
The 26-day parameter originally represented a full month of trading in Japan when the technique was invented, as trading then occurred on Saturdays.
Gerald Appel independently selected 26 days as an optimal parameter for the MACD indicator in the late 1970s based on extensive trials.
Goichi Hosoda validated the 26-day parameter in 1969 using manual backtesting performed by dozens of students.

fig_p443_101.png.title: Ichimoku Crossover Sell Signal Chart
fig_p443_101.png.description: A financial candlestick chart illustrating the Ichimoku Kinko Hyo technical indicator. It features price candles, the Tenkan-sen (red line), Kijun-sen (blue line), Chikou Span (purple line), and the shaded Kumo cloud (green and red areas). An arrow points to a 'Crossover Sell Signal' where the red line crosses below the blue line, preceding a downward price movement through the cloud.
knowledge_claims: A bearish crossover sell signal is generated when the faster Tenkan-sen (red line) crosses below the slower Kijun-sen (blue line).
The crossover signal shown occurs near the top of the cloud, preceding a sharp price decline below the cloud support level.

fig_p444_102.png.title: Ichimoku Chart with Crossover Sell Signal
fig_p444_102.png.description: A candlestick stock chart illustrating the Ichimoku Cloud indicator system during a downtrend. An arrow points to a 'Crossover Sell Signal' where the conversion line crosses below the base line near the green cloud, preceding a sharp price decline. The price subsequently moves below the red-shaded cloud, which acts as a resistance zone.
knowledge_claims: A bearish crossover of the Ichimoku lines near the cloud boundary serves as a sell signal.
After the crossover sell signal, the price breaks below the cloud, confirming a strong downward trend.
The shaded Kumo (cloud) acts as a dynamic resistance level during the subsequent bearish phase.

fig_p445_103.png.title: Ichimoku on 60-minute chart with stochastic oscillator
fig_p445_103.png.description: A financial candlestick chart showing a downward price trend overlaid with the Ichimoku Kinko Hyo indicator. A black circle and arrow point to the Chikou Span (lagging line) labeled 'Chikou'. Below the main price chart, a stochastic oscillator panel displays blue and orange lines oscillating between dotted horizontal boundaries, showing a crossover to the downside.
knowledge_claims: The Chikou Span represents the current price projected 26 periods backward and is used as a momentum/trend confirmation indicator.
The price action is trading below the red Ichimoku cloud (Kumo), which signifies a prevailing bearish trend.
The stochastic oscillator at the bottom shows a bearish crossover, indicating downward momentum in a sideways or transitioning market environment.

fig_p446_104.png.title: Ichimoku Chart with Linear Regression Channel
fig_p446_104.png.description: A candlestick stock chart showing a strong downward trend. It overlays the Ichimoku Kinko Hyo indicator, including the shaded cloud (Kumo) and lagging span, with a linear regression channel consisting of three parallel downward-sloping lines (blue, grey, and red). The price candles fluctuate mostly within this regression channel, finding resistance near the upper blue line and the red Ichimoku cloud.
knowledge_claims: The linear regression channel acts as a mathematically independent confirmation of the bearish trend indicated by the Ichimoku cloud.
The upper boundary of the linear regression channel aligns closely with the Ichimoku cloud resistance, reinforcing the bearish outlook.
Price action remains largely confined within the standard error bands of the downward-sloping regression channel.

fig_p446_105_vec.png.title: Adding Trading Rules Callout
fig_p446_105_vec.png.description: A text callout box titled 'ADDING TRADING RULES' that references Manesh Patel's book, 'Trading with Ichimoku Clouds, The Essential Guide to Ichimoku Kinko Hyo Technical Analysis'. It introduces a summary of specific trading rules, starting with the conditions for when to buy.
knowledge_claims: Manesh Patel's book 'Trading with Ichimoku Clouds' provides specific trading rules for Ichimoku Kinko Hyo technical analysis.
The section introduced by this callout outlines specific criteria for when a trader should buy based on Ichimoku indicators.

fig_p447_106_vec.png.title: Ichimoku Trading Rules and Backtesting Guidelines
fig_p447_106_vec.png.description: A text-based informational box detailing specific buy entry rules for the Ichimoku Kinko Hyo system as proposed by Manesh Patel. It lists five technical conditions involving the cloud, Tenkan, Kijun, and Chikou lines, and discusses the importance of backtesting, maintaining standard parameters, and adjusting stops daily.
knowledge_claims: To buy using Patel's Ichimoku rules, the price must be above the cloud, Tenkan must cross above Kijun, and Chikou must have significant space from the price.
Entry limits require the price to be less than 200 points from the Tenkan and 300 points from the Kijun.
Standard Ichimoku parameters (9-day, 26-day, and 52-day) should not be changed; instead, traders should define specific point or dollar distances for entry, stop, and exit points.
Adding specific trading rules to Ichimoku helps prevent failure but typically results in signal delay.
